qnaData.stem:
Rupesh, 8 years old child presented in ER of Paediatrics Department with complaint of abrupt onset of fever for 4 days, severe headache, vomiting, photophobia. There is Neck stiffness and Kernig’s sign positive on clinical examination.
a) What is the clinical diagnosis of Rupesh?
b) How will you investigate Rupesh?
c) Write treatment plan for Rupesh.
d) Write complications of the disease. (1+2+2+2=7)
SAQ. Enumerate Modified Jones Criteria. (5)
SAQ. Laws of Growth. (5)
SAQ. Vitamin D deficiency Rickets - clinical features and management. (5)
SAQ. Write treatment plan of some dehydration & composition of ORS. (5)

A 6 years old Rishi presents with generalized edema and hematuria in ER of Paediatrics Department. There was history of skin infection 3 weeks back. Child's B.P. was 140/90 mm Hg & healed pyoderma scars were present.
a) What is the most Probable diagnosis?
b) Write differential diagnosis for this condition.
c) Plan investigations for Rishi.
d) How will you manage Rishi? (1+2+3+2=8)

Ravi, 12 years old male presented with sudden appearance of bruises, epistaxis, mucosal bleeds, petechiae in paediatrics OPD. Ravi had one episode of febrile illness few days back. On presentation child is afebrile.
a) What is the diagnosis of Ravi?
b) What are the differential diagnoses of this disease?
c) How will you investigate Ravi?
d) Write treatment plan for Ravi. (1+2+2+2=7)
SAQ. Complications of enteric fever. (5)
SAQ. Breath holding spells. (5)
SAQ. Advantages of Breast milk. (5)
SAQ. Management of Acute Severe Asthma. (5)

Suman, 7 months old child was brought in ER of Paediatrics Department with breathing difficulty. Mother complaint that child is unable to suck properly and excessive sweating is seen over forehead while sucking. There are similar 2 episodes previously for which she was treated locally. On clinical examination weight is 4 Kg, RR=54/mt, HR=180/mt, Murmur is heard over left parasternal region and tender hepatomegaly.
a) Write clinical Diagnosis of Suman.
b) Write investigation plan for Suman.
c) Write the treatment plan.
d) How the disease severity is assessed? (1+2+3+2=8)

A 2 years old boy visited to AIIMS pediatrics emergency with complaints of fever, headache, vomiting and multiple episodes of seizure for last 2 days. On examination child was in altered sensorium, neck stiffness present & no organomegaly.
a) What is the most probable diagnosis?
b) How will you confirm your diagnosis?
c) Management.
d) Complications. (1+2+3+2=8)

10 Year female child came to AIIMS OPD with complaint of fever for 3 weeks, migratory pain and swelling in large joint for 2 weeks, palpitation & breathlessness for 5 days, swelling over both feet and diminished urine output for 2 days.
a) Write your provisional diagnosis?
b) Management.
c) Prevention strategy. (1+4+2=7)
SAQ. Composition of WHO low osmolarity ORS and its rationality for use. (5)
SAQ. Kawasaki Disease. (5)
SAQ. Scorpion sting - clinical feature & management. (5)
SAQ. UTI - management & complication. (5)

A 2 years old male child presented to OPD with complaints of pallor and past history of monthly blood transfusion since 6 months of age. On examination child was found to have severe pallor and hepatosplenomegaly.
a) What is the most probable diagnosis?
b) Which investigation you will order in this child and mention the findings of each investigations pertaining to diagnosis?
c) Write 2 important complications of this disease.
d) What will be your management plan? (1+3+1+3=8)

A 8 years boy visited to pediatric emergency with complaints of cough for 2 days and fast breathing since last 6 hours. Child had similar episode twice in last 1 year needing nebulization for relief of symptom on examination PR=120 bpm, spO2 - 91 % Room air chest- bilateral wheeze + .
a) What is your diagnosis?
b) What will be the investigation findings in these conditions?
c) Management of this child. (1+2+4=7)
SAQ. Explain phases/stages of classical pertussis. (5)
SAQ. What is development? Rules of development. (5)
SAQ. Difference between Nephrotic & Nephritic Syndrome. (5)
SAQ. Clinical Approach to a case of Rickets. (5)

A 6 months old boy with weight- 3.5 kg presented to AIIMS OPD with poor feeding, breathing difficulty for last 2 months. On examination, RR-62/min, no cyanosis, PR- 150/MIN, SPO2- 96 % RA. Per abdomen liver span-8 cm, CVS- pansystolic murmur heard in the left parasternal area. What is your most probable diagnosis? Describe briefly about the hemodynamics, complication & management of this condition. (1+3+1+3=8)

A 3 Year old girl came to emergency with complaints of fever on & off for 1 months, vomiting, lethargy for 15 days & abnormal movement of limbs with uprolling of eyeball for 3 days. On examination child was stuporous, neck rigidity present. What is you most common provisional diagnosis? Write down the clinical features (stages), investigation & treatment. (1+2+2+2=7)
SAQ. Classification of dehydration & management of severe dehydration. (2.5+2.5)
SAQ. Clinical features & complication of measles. (2.5+2.5)
SAQ. UTI - management & complication. (2+3)
SAQ. Down syndrome clinical feature, investigation & management. (2+1+2)

A 1 year 6 months child presented to emergency with fever & cough for 5 days, difficulty in breathing for last 2 days. On examination, child is drowsy, RR- 60/min, SPO 2 =90% in room air, RS Examination chest retraction, B/L chest crepitation are present. What is your provisional diagnosis? Write briefly the management, complications & role of vaccines in prevention. (1+3+2+1=7)

A 4 Years female child born out of a consanguineous marriage, brought to pediatric OPD with complaint of fever & multiple Joint pain of both upper & lower limbs for last 5 days with a history of similar episodes in the past. On examination child had severe pallor, mild icterus, on systemic examination mild hepatosplenomegaly was present. What is your most likely diagnosis? Write investigation findings & treatment. (1+3+4=8)

SAQ. A 5-year-old boy came to OPD with the complaint of oedema for 5 days. On examination, the patient had high BP and cola color urine. What is your provisional diagnosis? Briefly describe the management of the disease. (2+3)
SAQ. Physiology of lactation. (5)
SAQ. Pathological Jaundice- characteristics & differential diagnosis. (2+3)
SAQ. Rashtriya Bal swasthya karyakram (RBSK). (5)

A 13-year-old boy is brought by his parents due to concerns about his short height compared to peers. He is otherwise healthy, with no history of chronic illness, poor appetite, or developmental delay. His academic performance is good, and he actively participates in sports. The father mentions that he had a similar pattern of late growth and puberty, eventually attaining normal adult height. Anthropometric Measurements: Height: 135 cm (below 3rd percentile for age), Weight: 32 kg (10th percentile), Mid-parental height: 174 cm (target height range: 169-179 cm), Upper segment: Lower segment ratio: Normal for age, Arm span: 134 cm (normal), Height velocity: 4.5 cm/year (within normal range for age), Bone age (X-ray left wrist): 10 years (delayed by ~3 years), Pubertal status: Prepubertal (Tanner stage I)
a) Diagnosis with reasoning (1)
b) Describe the clinical approach (2)
c) Outline the investigations to differentiate various causes of the clinical scenario (3)
d) Management of this clinical condition (2)

A 2-year-old boy presented to the OPD with complaints of difficulty walking. On examination, the child had a broad-based gait with bow legs and thickened wrists. What is your provisional diagnosis? How will you investigate to confirm the diagnosis? What is your management Plan? (1+3+3)

SAQ. A 9-month-old infant is brought to the pediatric clinic with concerns of poor weight gain since 4 months of age. The child was born full-term with a birth weight of 3.2 kg. Exclusive breastfeeding was continued till 6 months, and weaning was started thereafter. The mother reports that the child is not interested in solid foods and frequently vomits after feeds. Developmental milestones are mildly delayed. On examination, the infant appears undernourished with reduced subcutaneous fat and muscle mass. Weight is 5.8 kg (below the 3rd percentile), length is 66 cm (between 5th-10th percentile), and head circumference is 43 cm (10th percentile).
a) Diagnosis (1)
b) List the possible causes in this child (2)
c) Outline the approach to evaluate this case (2)
SAQ. Breath holding Spell (5)

SAQ. A 7-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric outpatient clinic by his parents with complaints of bedwetting at night for the past 1 year. He has no history of daytime urinary symptoms, urinary tract infection or developmental delay. Physical examination and urinalysis are normal. His parents are concerned about the possible causes and treatment options. What is your diagnosis and how will you manage this patient? (1+4)

SAQ. A 3-year-old child is brought by his parents with concerns about delayed speech, lack of eye contact, and poor response to name. The parents also report that the child prefers to play alone and exhibits repetitive hand flapping movements. There is no history of hearing loss or significant perinatal issues. Developmental milestones have been otherwise normal. What is your diagnosis, and write about the disease's diagnostic criteria? (1+4)

A 2-year-old girl was brought to the Pediatrics OPD with complaints of swelling of the feet, irritability, poor appetite, and frequent infections. On examination, the child has bilateral pedal edema & wt/ht < -3SD (WHO).
a) What is the most likely diagnosis? (1)
b) Management (5)
c) Discharge indication & following plan (2)

A 2-year-old male child is brought by his mother with concerns that he is not able to speak any meaningful words and cannot walk without support. He was born at term by vaginal delivery, but had delayed crying at birth and required NICU admission for 10 days and having history of seizure during the course in NICU. On examination, he has increased tone in both lower limbs and a scissoring gait. His milestones are significantly delayed in all domains.
a) What is the likely diagnosis in this child? (1)
b) Discuss the differential diagnosis, clinical evaluation, and investigations in this case. (4)
c) Outline the principles of management (2)

SAQ. A 5-year-old girl is brought to the Pediatrics department with complaints of eating mud, soap and chalk for the past several months. The child appears pale on examination.
a) What is the most likely diagnosis? (1)
b) Discuss the complications and outline the management. (4)
qnaData.chapter: 51. Approach to Anemia in Children and Nutritional Anemia

qnaData.stem:
SAQ. A 28-year-old primigravida mother complains of difficulty in breastfeeding her 4-day-old baby. The child is irritable and not feeding well.
a) What are the common causes of difficulties during breastfeeding in early lactation? (1.5)
b) Discuss the benefits of exclusive breastfeeding for both the newborn and the mother. (2.5)
c) What advice would you give this mother to promote successful lactation? (1)
SAQ. WHO Classification of Xerophthalmia (5)

SAQ. A 10-year-old boy with chronic kidney disease presents with fatigue and muscle weakness. His ECG shows tall, peaked T waves and a widened QRS complex.
a) What is the cause? (1)
b) What are the treatment modalities for this presentation? (4)
qnaData.chapter: 38. Acute Kidney Injury and Chronic Kidney Disease

qnaData.stem:
A 2-year-old child presents with progressive pallor, poor weight gain, and increasing abdominal distension since infancy. The child has required repeated hospital admissions for severe anaemia. There is no history of bleeding. On examination, the child has marked pallor, mild icterus, frontal prominence, and hepatosplenomegaly. Hemogram shows haemoglobin 6 g/dL with reduced MCV and MCH. Peripheral smear reveals aniso-poikilocytosis with occasional nucleated red blood cells.
a) What is the most likely diagnosis? [1]
b) What specific investigation would you order to confirm the diagnosis? Describe the expected findings. [2]
c) Enumerate the important long-term complications of this disorder. [1.5]
d) Outline the principles of management and follow-up. [3]

A 7-year-old child presents with high-grade fever for 3 days. On examination, the child has a diffuse erythematous rash with a sandpaper-like texture, predominantly involving the flexor surfaces of the limbs. The tongue appears bright red with prominent papillae (strawberry tongue). There is associated sore throat. What is the most likely diagnosis and enumerate etiological agent? What is the management of the disease? Also, mention the complications of the disease? [2+3.5+2]
SAQ. Write a short note on Anaemia Mukt Bharat (AMB) program. [5]
qnaData.chapter: 51. Approach to Anemia in Children and Nutritional Anemia

qnaData.stem:
SAQ. A 6-year-old previously healthy child presents with sudden onset of petechiae and ecchymoses following a viral illness. There is no history of fever, bone pain, or weight loss. Examination reveals multiple petechiae over the limbs with no hepatosplenomegaly. Complete blood count shows 20,000 platelets with normal haemoglobin and white blood cell counts.
a) What is the most likely diagnosis? [1]
b) Mention two important differential diagnoses. [2]
c) How will you manage this child. [2]
SAQ. Describe the FAB classification of Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) and enumerate prognostic factors for ALL? [2.5+2.5]

SAQ. A 2-year-old child weighing 12kg with diarrhea is prescribed WHO-ORS. The mother asks why ORS works even though it does not reduce stool frequency.
a) Explain the physiological basis of WHO-ORS action. [3]
b) Calculate the fluid for the child assuming some dehydration? [2]

7-year-old male child presents to the Pediatric emergency department with a 5-day history of progressive jaundice, fever, vomiting, and altered sensorium. On examination, he is deeply comatosed (Glasgow Coma Scale 6/15), with hyperreflexia, upgoing plantars, and decerebrate posturing. There is clinical evidence of acute liver failure including hepatomegaly, ascites, and coagulopathy (INR 4.2). Laboratory investigations reveal total bilirubin 18 mg/dL, ALT 2800 IU/L, AST 3200 IU/L, ammonia 250 µg/dL, and normal platelet count with no evidence of fresh gastrointestinal bleeding. What is the probable diagnosis. Write the etiology, management and prognosis of this clinical scenario [1+2+3+1.5]

A 2 years boy with weight 6 kg and height 76 cm (Wt/Ht <-3SD) and bipedal edema presented to emergency with complaints of loose motion for 3 days with low grade fever. On examination child was lethargic with poor appetite and low BP. What is your provisional diagnosis? What are the complications encountered in these children? What will be your management strategy for this child. [1+ 3+3.5]

SAQ. A mother of a 13-year-old boy asks whether HPV vaccination is needed for males.
a) What are the indications of HPV vaccination in boys? [1.5]
b) What diseases are prevented by HPV vaccine and what is dosing schedule? [3.5]

SAQ. A 4-year-old child is found to be unimmunized and has no past history of jaundice.
a) What Hepatitis B vaccination schedule should be followed? [2]
b) Is pre-vaccination serological testing required? [1]
c) What is schedule of Hepatis-B vaccine in National immunization schedule (NIS)? [2]
SAQ. Childhood Obesity definition, investigations, management [1+ 1+3]
SAQ. Pathophysiology and Complications of Portal Hypertension [3+2]

qnaData.stem: A 1 month 15 days baby is bought to OPD with history of cough and coryza for 14 days, paroxysms of cough followed by posttussive vomiting. Child had no history of fever, myalgia and examination of respiratory system did not reveal any significant findings. What is the recommended treatment for this child?

A 6 years child was brought to emergency with history of fall 2 hours back with a grossly contaminated open wound in the left leg. Parents are more apprehensive about the risk of tetanus. Child has received all the routine vaccinations till 5 years of age. What advice you will give to their parents regarding tetanus prophylaxis?
options: Administer Tetanus toxoid single dose + Tetanus Immunoglobulin
Observe and start tetanus toxoid if signs and symptoms appear
